enrichment.short_description: Maza is a free, open-source Bash script that blocks ads and trackers system-wide on macOS, Linux, BSD and WSL by rewriting the operating system's own DNS resolution, with no router or extra device required.
enrichment.seo_title: Maza Ad Blocking Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Maza is a free, open-source Bash-based DNS ad blocker for macOS, Linux, BSD and WSL. See how it works, key features, pricing and top alternatives like Pi-hole.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Maza (maza-ad-blocking) is a free, open-source, single-file Bash script created by developer Andros Fenollosa, known as tanrax on GitHub, that blocks ads and trackers at the DNS level using only the host operating system's own DNS resolution tools. Unlike Pi-hole, which typically runs as a separate service on dedicated hardware such as a Raspberry Pi acting as a network-wide DNS sink, Maza runs locally on the same machine it protects, requiring no additional device, container, browser extension, or app installation. It is compatible with macOS, Linux, BSD, and Windows Subsystem for Linux (WSL). Maza works by downloading a curated list of ad- and tracker-serving domains and writing them into the operating system's DNS or hosts configuration, effectively blocking those domains for every browser and application on the machine at once rather than a single browser. By default, Maza uses the Yoyo (Peter Lowe) block list, which contains roughly 3,700 domains, but users can switch to more extensive lists such as Steven Black's hosts list, which blocks over 135,000 domains, by editing a single line in the script. Users can also append their own custom domains to a local configuration file to extend blocking beyond the default lists, and the entire block list can be refreshed with one command. The tool has no graphical interface, dashboard, or account system, reflecting a minimalist, command-line-first design aimed at developers, sysadmins, and privacy-conscious power users comfortable working in a terminal. The project is released under the Apache License 2.0 and is hosted both on GitHub (tanrax/maza-ad-blocking) and on the maintainer's self-hosted Git instance at git.andros.dev, with a marketing and documentation page at maza-ad-blocking.andros.dev. As a purely open-source utility with no company behind it, Maza has no funding, no enterprise tier, and no commercially driven roadmap; it is maintained on a volunteer, community basis. Its closest comparisons are Pi-hole and AdGuard Home, both of which provide similar DNS-based ad blocking but run as persistent network services, often on dedicated hardware or in Docker containers, that protect every device on a home network and include a web dashboard for statistics and management. Maza's key differentiation is simplicity: it is a lightweight script rather than a standing server, so it only protects the single machine it is installed on, but it requires none of the setup, port-forwarding, or hardware investment that Pi-hole demands. It is best suited to individual developers and privacy-conscious users who want fast, no-frills, script-based ad blocking on one laptop or desktop rather than centralized, network-wide protection for an entire household.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Maza is a lightweight, open-source ad blocker distributed as a single Bash script rather than a full application or service. It is designed to be a simpler alternative to Pi-hole for people who only need to protect one machine and do not want to set up a dedicated server, router change, or piece of hardware.

The tool works by rewriting DNS resolution at the operating system level, so once installed it blocks known ad and tracking domains for every browser and application on the machine, not just a single browser extension's scope.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Maza ships with the Yoyo (Peter Lowe) block list by default, covering roughly 3,700 known ad-serving domains, and can be reconfigured in one line to use larger community lists such as Steven Black's hosts file, which covers well over 135,000 domains.

Because it relies entirely on native OS tooling, Maza has no background daemon, browser extension, or account to manage. Custom domains can be added to a local config file, and the whole block list is refreshed with a single command, which keeps the tool easy to audit and modify for technically inclined users.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Maza is completely free and open source under the Apache License 2.0. There is no paid tier, subscription, or account system of any kind.

Because there is no company monetizing the project, users get the full functionality of the script at no cost, but should also expect community-level support rather than commercial SLAs or dedicated customer service.

features.title: DNS-level blocking
features.description: Blocks ad and tracking domains by rewriting the operating system's DNS resolution rather than relying on a browser extension.

features.title: No extra hardware or router setup
features.description: Runs directly on the protected machine using its own OS tools, unlike Pi-hole which typically needs a separate device.

features.title: Cross-platform support
features.description: Works on macOS, Linux, BSD, and Windows Subsystem for Linux (WSL).

features.title: System-wide protection
features.description: Blocks ads and trackers across every browser and application on the machine at once, not just one browser.

features.title: Switchable block lists
features.description: Ships with the Yoyo (Peter Lowe) list by default and can be switched to Steven Black's larger 135k-domain list with a one-line change.

features.title: Custom domain blocking
features.description: Users can add their own domains to a local configuration file to extend or personalize blocking.

features.title: One-command updates
features.description: The full block list can be refreshed with a single terminal command.

features.title: Zero dependencies
features.description: Distributed as a single Bash script with no external runtime, package manager, or installer required.

pros: Completely free and open source under the Apache License 2.0
No router, dedicated hardware, or network reconfiguration required
Extremely lightweight, a single Bash script rather than a running service
Blocks ads and trackers for every app on the machine, not just the browser
Easy to customize with personal domain blocklists
cons: No graphical interface or dashboard, command-line only
Only protects the single machine it is installed on, not a whole network
Very small, niche project with a much smaller community than Pi-hole or AdGuard Home
No official mobile app support
Documentation and support are community-driven with no commercial backing

faqs.question: What is Maza ad blocking?
faqs.answer: Maza is a free, open-source Bash script that blocks ads and trackers by rewriting DNS resolution on the local operating system, similar in concept to Pi-hole but without needing a separate device.

faqs.question: How is Maza different from Pi-hole?
faqs.answer: Pi-hole runs as a network-wide DNS server, typically on separate hardware, protecting every device on a network. Maza instead runs locally on a single machine using that machine's own DNS tools, so it only protects the device it is installed on.

faqs.question: Does Maza work in every browser?
faqs.answer: Yes, because it blocks domains at the DNS level rather than inside a single browser, Maza affects all browsers and any other software on the machine that makes network requests.

faqs.question: Is Maza free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Maza is free and open source under the Apache License 2.0, with no paid plans or subscriptions.

faqs.question: What operating systems does Maza support?
faqs.answer: Maza is documented to work on macOS, Linux, BSD, and Windows Subsystem for Linux (WSL).

faqs.question: Can I add my own blocklists to Maza?
faqs.answer: Yes, users can add custom domains to a local configuration file, and can also swap the default Yoyo list for larger third-party lists such as Steven Black's hosts file.

use_cases.title: Single-machine ad blocking for developers
use_cases.description: Provides DNS-level ad and tracker blocking on a personal laptop or workstation without needing a dedicated Pi-hole server.

use_cases.title: Lightweight privacy tool for terminal users
use_cases.description: Offers a no-GUI, script-based way to reduce ad and tracker exposure for users who prefer managing tools from the command line.

use_cases.title: Low-effort alternative to Pi-hole
use_cases.description: Useful for people who want basic DNS-based ad blocking without setting up and maintaining separate server hardware.
use_cases.audience: Home users without spare hardware for a dedicated ad-blocking server

features: Full DNS-level ad and tracker blocking
Optional switch to Steven Black's list (135k+ domains)
Works on macOS, Linux, BSD and WSL

enrichment.short_description: Maze is a continuous product discovery and user-testing platform that lets product, design, and research teams validate ideas, test prototypes, and gather user feedback at scale.
enrichment.seo_title: Maze Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Maze is a rapid user-testing and product research platform for validating ideas and prototypes. See its features, pricing plans, pros, cons and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Maze is a continuous product discovery and user-testing platform founded in 2018 by Jonathan Widawski and Thomas Mary and headquartered in San Francisco, California. It is built for product managers, UX researchers, and designers who need to validate ideas, test prototypes, and collect user feedback quickly without running full traditional research studies. The platform combines several research methods in one workspace: usability and prototype testing (including live imports from design tools such as Figma, Adobe XD, and Sketch), five-second tests, card sorting, tree testing, surveys, live website testing, and moderated interview studies. Maze also offers an AI moderator that can run and summarize conversational interviews, and AI-assisted analysis features that summarize open-ended responses and highlight patterns across studies, positioning the product as a way to compress weeks of manual research synthesis into hours. Teams can recruit participants from Maze's own testers panel or bring their own users, and results are compiled into shareable, stakeholder-friendly reports with metrics like task success rate, misclick rate, and time on task. Maze operates on a freemium model: a free plan supports basic testing with limited monthly response volume, while paid plans (commonly referenced as Starter and Organization tiers, plus a custom Enterprise tier) unlock higher participant limits, panel recruitment, interview studies, AI-powered analysis, and enterprise security and support features, with per-seat pricing that scales from roughly the ninety-dollar range per seat per month at the entry paid tier up into the low hundreds per seat at higher tiers, billed primarily on an annual basis. In 2025 the company raised a growth funding round (reported at roughly twenty-two million euros) from investors including Cherry Ventures, Tapestry VC, and Theory Ventures, extending a funding history that began with earlier venture rounds after its 2018 founding. Maze's core differentiation versus generic survey tools or design-file commenting is its focus on quantifiable usability metrics tied directly to prototypes and live products, combined with fast turnaround: studies can be built, distributed, and analyzed within the same platform rather than stitching together separate tools for recruiting, testing, and reporting. Its main competitors include UserTesting, Lookback, UsabilityHub, and, for some workflows, Optimal Workshop, with Maze generally positioned as faster and more design-tool-integrated than heavier enterprise research suites, while still offering panel access and reporting depth beyond lightweight survey tools. Typical customers span startups running lean product discovery cycles up through larger product organizations that need to run frequent, low-friction usability checks alongside more formal research programs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Maze is a product research and user-testing platform that helps teams get fast, quantifiable feedback on prototypes, live products, and early ideas. It is built around the idea of continuous product discovery, letting product managers, designers, and researchers run smaller, more frequent studies instead of relying only on occasional, large-scale research projects.

Founded in 2018 and based in San Francisco, Maze has grown into a platform used by product teams to combine usability testing, surveys, interviews, and analytics in a single workspace, with direct integrations into design tools such as Figma, Adobe XD, and Sketch.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Maze supports a range of research methods including prototype and usability testing, five-second tests, card sorting, tree testing, surveys, and live website testing, alongside moderated interview studies enhanced by an AI moderator that can conduct and summarize conversations at scale.

The platform includes participant recruitment through its own testers panel, automated reporting with usability metrics such as task success rate and misclick rate, and AI-assisted analysis that summarizes open-ended feedback and surfaces patterns, reducing the manual work typically required to synthesize research findings.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Maze offers a free plan with limited monthly usage suitable for small teams testing the platform, alongside paid Starter and Organization tiers priced per seat and billed primarily on an annual basis, plus a custom-priced Enterprise tier for larger organizations.

Paid tiers unlock higher response and participant limits, access to Maze's recruiting panel, interview studies, AI-powered analysis features, and enterprise-grade security, administration, and support, with per-seat costs increasing significantly between the entry paid tier and the top tiers.

features.title: Prototype and usability testing
features.description: Import prototypes directly from Figma, Adobe XD, or Sketch and test them with real users, capturing task success and completion metrics automatically.

features.title: AI moderator
features.description: Runs and summarizes conversational interview studies at scale, reducing the manual effort of moderating and transcribing user interviews.

features.title: Surveys and five-second tests
features.description: Supports quick quantitative and qualitative feedback methods for testing messaging, first impressions, and concepts.

features.title: Card sorting and tree testing
features.description: Helps teams validate information architecture and navigation structures before building them.

features.title: Live website testing
features.description: Tests usability directly on published, live websites and products, not just design prototypes.

features.title: Participant recruitment panel
features.description: Access to Maze's own tester panel for recruiting study participants, in addition to bringing your own users.

features.title: Automated reporting
features.description: Generates shareable reports with usability metrics like task success rate, misclick rate, and time on task.

features.title: AI-assisted analysis
features.description: Summarizes open-ended responses and surfaces patterns across studies to speed up research synthesis.

pros: Combines multiple research methods, prototype testing, surveys, interviews, and live testing, in one platform
Strong integrations with design tools like Figma, Adobe XD, and Sketch
AI moderator and AI analysis features speed up research synthesis
Built-in participant recruitment panel removes the need for a separate recruiting tool
Reports are designed to be shareable and easy for non-researchers to understand
cons: Free plan has limited monthly response and testing volume
Per-seat pricing on paid tiers can get expensive for larger teams
Some advanced features like interview studies and panel recruitment are gated to higher-priced tiers
Less suited to deep, long-form ethnographic or in-person research compared to specialized research tools
AI-generated summaries still require human review for nuanced findings

faqs.question: What is Maze used for?
faqs.answer: Maze is used by product, design, and research teams to test prototypes, run surveys, conduct interviews, and gather usability feedback from real users before and after launch.

faqs.question: Is Maze free to use?
faqs.answer: Maze offers a free plan with limited monthly usage, alongside paid Starter, Organization, and custom Enterprise plans that unlock higher limits and additional features.

faqs.question: Does Maze integrate with Figma?
faqs.answer: Yes, Maze integrates directly with Figma as well as Adobe XD and Sketch, allowing teams to import and test prototypes without leaving their design workflow.

faqs.question: Where is Maze headquartered?
faqs.answer: Maze is headquartered in San Francisco, California, and was founded in 2018.

faqs.question: How is Maze different from UserTesting?
faqs.answer: Maze focuses on fast, self-serve, prototype-centric usability testing with strong design-tool integrations and AI-assisted analysis, while UserTesting is a broader, more enterprise-oriented research platform with a larger participant network and more formal research workflows.

faqs.question: Can Maze recruit test participants for me?
faqs.answer: Yes, Maze provides access to its own panel of testers for recruiting study participants, in addition to allowing teams to invite their own users.

use_cases.title: Rapid prototype validation
use_cases.description: Product and design teams test Figma or Adobe XD prototypes with real users before development begins, catching usability issues early.

use_cases.title: Continuous product discovery
use_cases.description: Product managers run frequent, lightweight studies alongside the product roadmap to validate features and reduce build risk.

use_cases.title: Post-launch usability checks
use_cases.description: Teams test live websites and products to catch usability regressions and gather ongoing customer feedback.
use_cases.audience: Growth and UX teams at SaaS companies

features: Custom usage limits and contract terms

enrichment.short_description: McAfee is a long-established consumer and business cybersecurity company offering antivirus, VPN, identity protection, and password management software under brands like McAfee+ and Total Protection.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (limited antivirus scan) or from about $29.99 for the first year on paid plans
enrichment.seo_title: McAfee Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: McAfee offers antivirus, VPN, and identity protection software for consumers and businesses. Compare McAfee's features, pricing plans, pros, cons and rivals.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: McAfee is one of the oldest and most recognized names in cybersecurity, founded in 1987 in Santa Clara, California by John McAfee, who released one of the first commercial antivirus programs. John McAfee resigned from the company in 1994. In 1997 the company merged with Network General, PGP Corporation, and Helix Software to form Network Associates, before later reverting to the McAfee name. In 2010, Intel acquired McAfee in a deal valued at roughly $7.68 billion and rebranded it as Intel Security; in 2017 Intel spun the security business back out as an independent company again called McAfee, backed by private equity firm TPG. McAfee went public again in 2020 and was subsequently taken private in 2022 through a deal led by investment firms Advent International and Permira valued at roughly $14 billion. Today McAfee is headquartered in San Jose, California, and focuses primarily on consumer cybersecurity, offering products under the McAfee+ and Total Protection brands that bundle antivirus and malware scanning, a VPN, a password manager, identity theft monitoring and remediation support, web protection browser tools (WebAdvisor), and parental controls, sold as subscriptions covering anywhere from a single device up to ten or more devices per household. McAfee also maintains a separate enterprise security business serving businesses with endpoint protection and threat intelligence products, though its consumer antivirus and identity protection suite is its most widely recognized offering. Pricing for consumer plans is typically structured with a discounted introductory first-year price that renews at a higher standard rate; for example, entry-level Total Protection plans have been advertised starting around $29.99 for the first year with renewal pricing considerably higher, while mid-tier plans covering more devices and adding VPN or identity protection features cost more. McAfee's target audience spans everyday consumers seeking all-in-one device and identity protection, families needing multi-device and parental-control coverage, and, through its separate enterprise offerings, businesses needing endpoint security. Its main competitors in the consumer security space include Norton (Gen Digital), Bitdefender, Kaspersky, Avast, and Malwarebytes. McAfee differentiates itself with a long operating history, broad brand recognition, frequent pre-installation partnerships with PC manufacturers and ISPs, and an increasingly bundled approach that combines antivirus with VPN and identity protection in a single subscription rather than selling them as separate products.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: McAfee is a cybersecurity company founded in 1987 by John McAfee, credited with releasing one of the earliest commercial antivirus programs. Over nearly four decades the company has changed ownership and structure multiple times, including a period as part of Intel and a stint as a public company, before being taken private again in 2022.

Today McAfee is primarily known for its consumer security suites, sold under the McAfee+ and Total Protection brands, which bundle antivirus protection with a VPN, identity monitoring, and a password manager into a single subscription.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: McAfee's core offering is real-time antivirus and malware scanning, complemented by a firewall, web protection tools that flag risky links and sites (WebAdvisor), and a built-in VPN for encrypting internet traffic on public networks.

Higher-tier plans add identity theft monitoring and restoration support, credit monitoring in some markets, a password manager, parental controls, and coverage for multiple devices across Windows, macOS, Android, and iOS.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: McAfee sells tiered consumer subscriptions, commonly Basic, Plus, Premium, and Advanced, that scale by the number of covered devices and the depth of identity protection and VPN features included.

Plans are typically sold with a discounted introductory price for the first year, after which they renew at a higher standard annual rate, a pricing pattern common across the consumer antivirus industry.

features.title: Real-time antivirus and malware protection
features.description: Scans for and removes viruses, ransomware, spyware, and other malware in real time across supported devices.

features.title: Built-in VPN
features.description: Encrypts internet traffic, particularly useful on public Wi-Fi, included with most paid McAfee plans.

features.title: Identity theft protection and monitoring
features.description: Monitors for exposure of personal information and provides restoration support if identity theft occurs on higher-tier plans.

features.title: Password manager
features.description: Stores and autofills credentials securely across devices as part of bundled security suites.

features.title: Web protection (WebAdvisor)
features.description: Browser extension that flags risky websites, links, and downloads before users interact with them.

features.title: Parental controls
features.description: Lets families monitor and manage children's device usage and online activity.

features.title: Multi-device coverage
features.description: Single subscriptions can cover multiple devices across Windows, macOS, Android, and iOS.

features.title: Business and enterprise security
features.description: Separate enterprise product line offers endpoint protection and threat intelligence for organizations.

pros: Long operating history and strong brand recognition in cybersecurity
Bundles antivirus, VPN, identity protection, and password management in one subscription
Broad multi-device and cross-platform support
Frequently pre-installed or bundled by PC manufacturers and ISPs, easing initial adoption
Offers identity theft monitoring and restoration support on higher tiers
cons: Renewal pricing after the introductory first year can be significantly higher
Historically criticized for aggressive upsells and background resource usage on some devices
Some advanced features like identity protection are gated behind higher-priced tiers
Frequent ownership and rebranding changes over its history can create confusion about current offerings
Free version offers only very limited protection compared to paid competitors

faqs.question: Who founded McAfee?
faqs.answer: McAfee was founded in 1987 by John McAfee, who released one of the earliest commercial antivirus programs.

faqs.question: Is McAfee still owned by John McAfee?
faqs.answer: No, John McAfee resigned from the company in 1994. The company has since changed hands multiple times, including a period as part of Intel, and is now privately owned following a 2022 acquisition led by Advent International and Permira.

faqs.question: What is included in McAfee Total Protection?
faqs.answer: McAfee Total Protection bundles antivirus and malware scanning with a VPN, password manager, and identity theft protection features, with the exact feature set varying by plan tier.

faqs.question: Does McAfee offer a free version?
faqs.answer: McAfee offers limited free tools and trial scans, but full real-time protection, VPN, and identity features require a paid subscription.

faqs.question: How does McAfee pricing work?
faqs.answer: McAfee typically sells introductory first-year pricing that renews at a higher standard annual rate, with cost increasing by plan tier and number of covered devices.

faqs.question: What are the main alternatives to McAfee?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include Norton (Gen Digital), Bitdefender, Kaspersky, Avast, and Malwarebytes.

use_cases.description: Individuals and families use McAfee to cover multiple devices with antivirus, VPN, and web protection in a single subscription.

use_cases.title: Identity theft monitoring

enrichment.short_description: Medama Analytics is a free, open-source, self-hostable website analytics platform that tracks visitors without cookies or IP addresses using a tracker script under 1KB.
enrichment.seo_title: Medama Analytics Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Medama Analytics is a free, open-source, privacy-focused website analytics tool with a sub-1KB tracker and no cookies. See features, self-hosting and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Medama Analytics is an open-source, self-hostable website analytics platform maintained by the medama-io organization on GitHub, built for developers and privacy-conscious site owners who want to measure traffic without relying on cookies, IP address storage, or other personally identifying data. The project's tracker script is deliberately kept under 1KB in size to minimize its impact on page load performance, and it is designed to help sites comply with privacy regulations such as GDPR and PECR without needing a cookie consent banner. Architecturally, Medama's backend is written in Go and exposes an OpenAPI-documented REST API, using SQLite and DuckDB for data storage and processing, while the dashboard is a React and TypeScript single-page application that renders real-time charts and tables of visitor activity. The system is distributed as a single, dependency-free binary, and the project states it can run on virtual machines with as little as 256MB of memory for most small websites, making it comparatively cheap to self-host relative to heavier analytics stacks. Licensing is split: the core Go API and the dashboard are released under the Apache License 2.0, while the small tracker JavaScript snippet embedded on customer websites is released under the MIT License, a common pattern for open-source analytics tools that want the client-side script to be maximally permissive. Medama does not appear to offer an official managed hosting or SaaS tier at this time; it is positioned purely as a self-hosted, do-it-yourself analytics tool, with a live demo available at demo.medama.io and documentation at oss.medama.io. As a smaller project, it has a modest but active open-source community on GitHub. Medama sits in the same category as other privacy-first, self-hostable web analytics tools such as Umami, Plausible Analytics, Fathom Analytics, and Simple Analytics, all of which position themselves as lightweight, cookie-free alternatives to Google Analytics. Medama's particular differentiation is its emphasis on being extremely lightweight and simple to deploy as a single binary with minimal external dependencies, combined with a dual-database approach using SQLite for smaller deployments and DuckDB for analytical query performance, plus a fully documented OpenAPI surface that makes it straightforward to integrate analytics data into custom dashboards or internal tools. It is best suited to developers and small-to-medium website operators who want full data ownership and minimal infrastructure overhead rather than a hosted, subscription-based analytics service.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Medama Analytics is an open-source website analytics tool designed to be self-hosted, giving site owners full control and ownership of their visitor data. It targets developers and organizations that want to measure site traffic without using cookies or storing IP addresses, helping simplify privacy compliance.

The project is maintained on GitHub under the medama-io organization and is distributed as a single, dependency-free binary alongside a lightweight client-side tracker script, making it straightforward to deploy on a small VM or container.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Medama's tracker script is kept under 1KB and avoids collecting cookies, IP addresses, or other identifying data, aiming for compliance with privacy regulations like GDPR and PECR without a cookie consent banner. The backend, written in Go, uses SQLite and DuckDB for storage and analytical queries, while an OpenAPI-documented REST API makes it easy to pull analytics data into custom tools.

The dashboard is a React and TypeScript application offering real-time charts and tables for exploring visitor behavior. The project reports that it can run on machines with as little as 256MB of memory, making it inexpensive to self-host for most small to medium websites.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Medama Analytics is free and open source. The core API and dashboard are licensed under Apache License 2.0, while the tracker script is licensed under the MIT License.
There is no known official managed hosting or paid SaaS tier; users are expected to self-host the software on their own infrastructure, similar to other open-source analytics tools in its category.

features.description: Tracks visitor activity without setting cookies or storing IP addresses, simplifying privacy compliance.

features.title: Sub-1KB tracker script

No cookies or IP address storage, simplifying privacy compliance

Fully documented OpenAPI REST API for custom integrations

Smaller community and ecosystem compared to Umami or Plausible
Documentation and long-term support depend on an open-source community rather than a company
Feature set is more limited than commercial, full-featured analytics platforms
No confirmed enterprise support or SLA options

faqs.question: What is Medama Analytics?
faqs.answer: Medama Analytics is an open-source, self-hostable website analytics platform that tracks visitors without cookies or IP addresses, using a lightweight tracker under 1KB in size.

faqs.question: Is Medama Analytics free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Medama is free and open source, with the core API and dashboard licensed under Apache License 2.0 and the tracker script under the MIT License.

faqs.question: Does Medama Analytics require cookie consent banners?
faqs.answer: Medama is designed to avoid using cookies and does not store IP addresses, which is intended to help sites comply with privacy laws like GDPR and PECR without needing a cookie banner.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Medama Analytics?
faqs.answer: Yes, Medama is built specifically to be self-hosted as a single, dependency-free binary, and the project states it can run on machines with as little as 256MB of memory.

faqs.question: What technology does Medama Analytics use?
faqs.answer: The backend is written in Go and uses SQLite and DuckDB for storage and analytical processing, while the dashboard is built with React and TypeScript.

faqs.question: How does Medama Analytics compare to Umami or Plausible?
faqs.answer: Like Umami and Plausible, Medama is a privacy-first, cookie-free analytics alternative to Google Analytics. Medama differentiates itself with an especially lightweight tracker, low memory requirements, and a dual SQLite and DuckDB storage approach.

use_cases.title: Privacy-compliant website analytics
use_cases.description: Site owners use Medama to measure traffic without collecting cookies or IP addresses, easing GDPR and PECR compliance.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted analytics for small infrastructure
use_cases.description: Developers deploy Medama on low-resource VMs or containers to avoid the cost and complexity of larger analytics platforms.

use_cases.title: Custom analytics integrations
use_cases.description: Teams use Medama's OpenAPI-documented REST API to pull visitor data into internal dashboards or tools.

enrichment.short_description: MediaCMS is a free, open-source video and media content management system built with Django and React that lets organizations run their own self-hosted video portal.
enrichment.seo_title: MediaCMS Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: MediaCMS is a free, open-source video and media CMS built on Django and React. Compare its features, self-hosting options, pricing and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: MediaCMS is an open-source video and media content management system that lets organizations build and run their own self-hosted video and media portal, comparable in purpose to a self-hosted YouTube or Vimeo. Work on the project began in mid-2018, led primarily by developers Markos Gogoulos, who built the Python and Django backend, and Yiannis (Giannis) Stergiou, who built the React and JavaScript frontend. In 2020, the digital rights and media organization EngageMedia became MediaCMS's Product Development Partner, supporting its ongoing development, particularly for use cases in independent and community media. The platform is released under the GNU Affero General Public License v3.0 (AGPLv3), a copyleft license that requires modifications made to hosted, publicly accessible deployments to also be made available as open source. Technically, MediaCMS is built on Django and React with a REST API, and it can be deployed either via Docker Compose or through an automation script that installs and configures all required services on a server. The platform supports video, audio, image, and PDF media types, with features including multiple publishing workflows (public, private, unlisted, and custom visibility), role-based access control connecting users to groups with view and edit permissions on their media, automatic transcription powered by a locally-run instance of OpenAI's Whisper speech recognition model, in-browser video editing tools for trimming, replacing, and creating segments from uploaded video, and a customized video.js-based player supporting multiple resolutions and playback speeds. Because MediaCMS is free and open source, there is no license fee for self-hosting; the maintaining team instead offers paid professional services including custom installations, development of additional functionality, migration from legacy platforms, integrations, training, and support, priced on a custom basis per engagement. Third-party managed hosting providers, such as Elestio, also offer MediaCMS as a hosted service with pricing starting from roughly $11 per month for basic instances, though this is not an official MediaCMS product. MediaCMS is aimed at organizations that need to run their own branded, privacy-controlled video and media platform rather than relying on YouTube, Vimeo, or other third-party hosts, including universities, media organizations, community and non-profit groups, and businesses with internal video libraries. Its main open-source alternatives include PeerTube, another self-hosted, federated video platform, as well as commercial enterprise video platforms like Kaltura and Brightcove, and general-purpose self-hosted media servers. MediaCMS differentiates itself with its modern Django and React stack, integrated in-browser video editing, and built-in automatic transcription, aiming to give self-hosted deployments feature parity closer to commercial video platforms than older open-source alternatives.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: MediaCMS is an open-source content management system purpose-built for video and other media types, letting organizations run their own self-hosted video and media portal. Development began in mid-2018, led by Markos Gogoulos on the backend and Yiannis Stergiou on the frontend, with EngageMedia joining as a Product Development Partner in 2020.

The platform is released under the GNU Affero General Public License v3.0, and is built using Django for the backend and React for the frontend, with a REST API supporting integrations and custom clients.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: MediaCMS supports video, audio, image, and PDF uploads, multiple publishing workflows for controlling visibility, and role-based access control that connects users to groups with defined view and edit permissions on media items.

The platform includes automatic transcription through a locally run Whisper integration, in-browser video editing to trim, replace, or create new segments from uploaded footage, and a customized video.js player with multiple resolution and playback speed options.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: MediaCMS itself is free and open source, with no license fee to self-host on your own infrastructure. Organizations that need help deploying or customizing it can purchase professional services directly from the MediaCMS team, including custom installations, feature development, migrations, integrations, training, and support.

Third-party managed hosting providers also offer MediaCMS as a hosted service, with entry-level pricing around $11 per month reported by at least one provider, though this is separate from the official open-source project.

features.title: Multi-format media support
features.description: Supports video, audio, image, and PDF content within a single platform.

features.title: Flexible publishing workflows
features.description: Content can be published as public, private, unlisted, or under custom visibility rules.

features.title: Role-based access control
features.description: Connects users to groups with defined view and edit permissions on media items.

features.title: Automatic transcription
features.description: Generates transcripts using a locally run Whisper speech recognition integration.

features.title: In-browser video editing
features.description: Lets users trim, replace, or create new segments from uploaded video directly in the browser.

features.title: Customized video player
features.description: Uses a video.js-based player supporting multiple resolutions and playback speeds.

features.title: REST API
features.description: Exposes a REST API for integrations and building custom clients or workflows.

features.title: Self-hosted deployment options
features.description: Can be deployed via Docker Compose or an automation script that provisions all required services on a server.

pros: Free and open source under the AGPLv3 license
Full control and ownership of hosted video and media content
Modern tech stack built on Django and React with a REST API
Built-in automatic transcription and in-browser video editing
Flexible publishing and access control workflows suited to organizations of varying sizes
cons: Requires self-hosting expertise and server infrastructure to run and maintain
AGPLv3 license requires derivative hosted services to also be open sourced
No official managed SaaS offering directly from the MediaCMS project
Smaller community and ecosystem compared to platforms like PeerTube
Professional support and custom development require a paid services engagement

faqs.question: What is MediaCMS used for?
faqs.answer: MediaCMS is used to build and run a self-hosted video and media portal, similar in purpose to a private YouTube or Vimeo, for organizations that want to control their own media hosting.

faqs.question: Is MediaCMS free?
faqs.answer: Yes, MediaCMS is free and open source under the GNU Affero General Public License v3.0. There is no license fee to self-host it.

faqs.question: What technology stack does MediaCMS use?
faqs.answer: MediaCMS is built with Django (Python) on the backend and React (JavaScript) on the frontend, exposing a REST API.

faqs.question: Does MediaCMS support automatic transcription?
faqs.answer: Yes, MediaCMS integrates with a locally run instance of the Whisper speech recognition model to generate automatic transcripts for uploaded video and audio.

faqs.question: How do I deploy MediaCMS?
faqs.answer: MediaCMS can be deployed using Docker Compose, or via an automation script that installs and configures all required services on a server.

faqs.question: What is the difference between MediaCMS and PeerTube?
faqs.answer: Both are open-source, self-hosted video platforms. MediaCMS is built on Django and React with features like in-browser editing and Whisper-based transcription, while PeerTube is built around federation using the ActivityPub protocol to share content across servers.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted organizational video portal
use_cases.description: Organizations deploy MediaCMS to host internal or public video libraries without relying on third-party platforms like YouTube.

use_cases.title: Community and non-profit media hosting
use_cases.description: Community media groups use MediaCMS, supported by partners like EngageMedia, to publish and manage independent media content.

use_cases.title: Internal training and knowledge video libraries
use_cases.description: Businesses use MediaCMS to organize and control access to internal training videos and media assets.

features: Video, audio, image and PDF support

enrichment.short_description: MediaMTX is a free, open-source, zero-dependency media server and proxy that reads, publishes, records, and converts real-time video and audio streams across protocols like RTSP, RTMP, WebRTC, SRT, and HLS.
enrichment.seo_title: MediaMTX Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: MediaMTX is a free, open-source media server supporting RTSP, RTMP, WebRTC, SRT and HLS. See its features, how it works, licensing and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: MediaMTX is a free, open-source, zero-dependency media server and proxy distributed as a single executable that can publish, read, proxy, record, and play back real-time video and audio streams. The project was originally created and published as rtsp-simple-server, reflecting its initial scope as a simple RTSP server, but as its protocol support and use cases expanded well beyond RTSP, it was renamed to MediaMTX to better reflect its role as a general-purpose media router or media broker rather than a protocol-specific tool. The maintainer describes MediaMTX's role as routing media streams from one source or protocol to another, and the software is maintained under the bluenviron GitHub organization. It is released under the MIT License, making it free to use, modify, and redistribute, including in commercial products. MediaMTX supports a broad set of streaming protocols, including Media-over-QUIC, SRT, WebRTC, RTSP, RTMP, Low-Latency HLS (LL-HLS), MPEG-TS, and RTP, and it can automatically convert a stream published in one protocol into any of the others it supports, letting a single camera or encoder feed be consumed by clients expecting different protocols. It integrates with common tools in the streaming ecosystem such as FFmpeg, OBS Studio, GStreamer, and standard web browsers for WebRTC and HLS playback. Beyond basic relaying, MediaMTX supports recording streams to disk in fMP4 or MPEG-TS formats, offers a Control API for programmatic management of the server, and exposes Prometheus-compatible metrics for monitoring stream health and server performance. Authentication can be configured internally, via HTTP callbacks, or via JWT tokens, giving operators flexibility in how they secure publishing and playback endpoints. Because it ships as a single, dependency-free binary that runs on Linux, Windows, and macOS, MediaMTX is commonly used to build video surveillance systems, IP camera relays, live streaming backends, and IoT and robotics video pipelines where lightweight, protocol-flexible media routing is needed without the overhead of a full media server stack like Wowza or a cloud-only service. As an actively maintained, community-driven open-source project rather than a commercial company, MediaMTX has no pricing tiers, support contracts, or company behind it in the traditional sense; users rely on the GitHub repository, documentation, and community for support. Its closest comparisons include other open-source streaming servers such as Wowza Streaming Engine (commercial), Nginx with the RTMP module, SRS (Simple Realtime Server), and Janus WebRTC Gateway, with MediaMTX differentiating itself through its unusually broad multi-protocol support in a single, zero-dependency binary.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: MediaMTX is an open-source media server and proxy that acts as a router for real-time video and audio streams, letting streams published in one protocol be read back in another. It began life as rtsp-simple-server, a lightweight RTSP-focused server, and was renamed to MediaMTX once its protocol support and use cases grew well beyond RTSP.
The project is maintained on GitHub under the bluenviron organization and released under the MIT License, making it free to use and modify, including in commercial deployments.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: MediaMTX supports a wide range of streaming protocols including Media-over-QUIC, SRT, WebRTC, RTSP, RTMP, LL-HLS, MPEG-TS, and RTP, and can automatically convert a stream from one protocol to another so a single source can serve clients with different requirements.

The server can record streams in fMP4 or MPEG-TS formats, exposes a Control API for programmatic management, and provides Prometheus-compatible metrics for monitoring, along with configurable internal, HTTP, or JWT-based authentication.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: MediaMTX is completely free and open source under the MIT License, with no paid tiers, licensing fees, or commercial entity behind it.
Because it ships as a single, dependency-free binary, operating costs are limited to the infrastructure running it, making it an inexpensive option compared to commercial media server products.

features.title: Multi-protocol streaming support
features.description: Supports Media-over-QUIC, SRT, WebRTC, RTSP, RTMP, LL-HLS, MPEG-TS, and RTP in a single server.

features.title: Automatic protocol conversion
features.description: Converts streams published in one protocol into any other supported protocol automatically.

features.title: Stream recording
features.description: Records incoming streams to disk in fMP4 or MPEG-TS format.

features.title: Control API
features.description: Provides an API for programmatically managing streams and server configuration.

features.title: Prometheus metrics
features.description: Exposes server and stream metrics in a format compatible with Prometheus monitoring.

features.title: Flexible authentication
features.description: Supports internal, HTTP-based, and JWT authentication for securing publish and read endpoints.

features.title: Zero-dependency single binary
features.description: Distributed as a single executable with no external dependencies, running on Linux, Windows, and macOS.

features.title: Broad tool compatibility
features.description: Works with FFmpeg, OBS Studio, GStreamer, and standard web browsers.

pros: Completely free and open source under the permissive MIT License
Supports an unusually wide range of streaming protocols in one server
Zero-dependency single binary, simple to deploy on Linux, Windows, or macOS
Automatic protocol conversion reduces the need for separate transcoding tools
Actively maintained with an API and Prometheus metrics for production use
cons: No commercial support contracts or SLAs available
Requires familiarity with streaming protocols and server configuration
No built-in graphical management dashboard out of the box
Community-driven support only, via GitHub issues and documentation
Scaling to very high concurrent stream counts requires manual infrastructure planning

faqs.question: What is MediaMTX used for?
faqs.answer: MediaMTX is used as a media server and proxy to publish, read, record, and convert real-time video and audio streams across protocols like RTSP, RTMP, WebRTC, SRT, and HLS.

faqs.question: Is MediaMTX free?
faqs.answer: Yes, MediaMTX is free and open source under the MIT License, with no paid tiers or licensing fees.

faqs.question: What was MediaMTX previously called?
faqs.answer: MediaMTX was originally released as rtsp-simple-server before being renamed once its protocol support expanded beyond RTSP.

faqs.question: What protocols does MediaMTX support?
faqs.answer: MediaMTX supports Media-over-QUIC, SRT, WebRTC, RTSP, RTMP, LL-HLS, MPEG-TS, and RTP.

faqs.question: Can MediaMTX record streams?
faqs.answer: Yes, MediaMTX can record incoming streams to disk in fMP4 or MPEG-TS formats.

faqs.question: Does MediaMTX work with FFmpeg and OBS Studio?
faqs.answer: Yes, MediaMTX is designed to integrate with common streaming tools including FFmpeg, OBS Studio, and GStreamer, as well as standard web browsers.

use_cases.title: IP camera and video surveillance relay
use_cases.description: Organizations use MediaMTX to relay and record streams from IP cameras across different protocols to viewing clients.

use_cases.title: Live streaming backend
use_cases.description: Developers use MediaMTX as a lightweight backend to ingest and distribute live streams for web or app-based viewing.

use_cases.title: IoT and robotics video pipelines
use_cases.description: Teams use MediaMTX to route low-latency video feeds between devices, robots, and monitoring dashboards.

features: Support for RTSP, RTMP, WebRTC, SRT, LL-HLS, MPEG-TS and RTP
Stream recording in fMP4 or MPEG-TS

enrichment.short_description: MediaWiki is the free, open-source wiki software originally built for Wikipedia and now maintained by the Wikimedia Foundation, powering Wikipedia and thousands of other wikis worldwide.
enrichment.headquarters: San Francisco, California, United States (Wikimedia Foundation)
languages: and hundreds of other languages via localization
enrichment.seo_title: MediaWiki Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: MediaWiki is the free, open-source wiki software that powers Wikipedia. Explore its features, extensions, licensing, and top alternatives for building wikis.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: MediaWiki is free, open-source wiki software written in PHP that was originally developed to run Wikipedia and now powers most of the Wikimedia Foundation's projects, including Wikipedia, Wiktionary, Wikimedia Commons, Wikiquote, Wikivoyage, Meta-Wiki, and Wikidata, as well as thousands of independent third-party wikis across the internet. Wikipedia itself launched in January 2001 running on an earlier Perl-based engine called UseModWiki. In January 2002, Magnus Manske developed a new PHP-based script to replace it, and later that year Lee Daniel Crocker rewrote the software again, calling the new version 'Phase III'; this rewrite was deployed to the English Wikipedia in July 2002 and forms the direct ancestor of modern MediaWiki. The Wikimedia Foundation was announced on June 20, 2003 to formally support Wikipedia and its sister projects, and shortly afterward, in July 2003, contributor Daniel Mayer suggested naming the software 'MediaWiki' as a play on 'Wikimedia.' The name was phased in starting in August 2003, and the first officially versioned release, MediaWiki 1.1, shipped in December 2003. Since then, development has been coordinated primarily by the Wikimedia Foundation along with a large volunteer developer community, with the software released under the GNU General Public License version 2 or later (GPLv2+), a copyleft open-source license. Architecturally, MediaWiki is built in PHP with a MySQL or MariaDB-backed database by default, and it uses a lightweight markup language called wikitext for page authoring, alongside built-in revision history tracking, discussion pages, granular user permissions, and full-text search. Its functionality is heavily extended through a large ecosystem of official and community-built extensions and skins; notable examples include the Wikibase extension introduced in 2012, which added support for structured data and underpins Wikidata, the VisualEditor extension launched in 2013, which added a WYSIWYG editing interface as an alternative to raw wikitext editing, and CirrusSearch, which integrates Elasticsearch for advanced full-text search across large wikis. Because MediaWiki is free and open source with no licensing cost, organizations self-host it on their own infrastructure or use third-party MediaWiki hosting providers, and the Wikimedia Foundation itself, headquartered in San Francisco, California, operates it at massive scale to serve Wikipedia's global traffic. MediaWiki's target audience spans the Wikimedia projects themselves, corporate and internal knowledge bases, community and fan wikis, government and academic documentation sites, and any organization that needs a collaboratively editable, version-controlled knowledge repository. Its main alternatives include Confluence (commercial, Atlassian), DokuWiki, TikiWiki, Notion (as a more modern collaborative workspace), and BookStack, with MediaWiki differentiating itself primarily through its proven ability to scale to Wikipedia's traffic levels, its very mature extension ecosystem, and its status as free, community-governed software rather than a commercial SaaS product.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: MediaWiki is the free, open-source wiki software that powers Wikipedia and most of the Wikimedia Foundation's sister projects. It grew out of earlier wiki engines used in Wikipedia's first years, was rewritten in PHP in 2002, and took on the MediaWiki name in 2003 as the Wikimedia Foundation was established to support it.

Since its first official release, MediaWiki 1.1, in December 2003, it has been continuously developed by the Wikimedia Foundation together with a large open-source community, and is now used by thousands of wikis beyond the Wikimedia projects themselves.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: MediaWiki is built around a lightweight wikitext markup language for content authoring, with full revision history, discussion pages, granular user permissions, and built-in full-text search. Its functionality is significantly extended through a large ecosystem of official and community extensions and skins.

Notable extensions include Wikibase, introduced in 2012 to support structured data and power Wikidata, VisualEditor, launched in 2013 to provide a WYSIWYG editing experience, and CirrusSearch, which integrates Elasticsearch for advanced search across large-scale wikis.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: MediaWiki is free and open source under the GNU General Public License version 2 or later, with no licensing fees to install, run, or modify it.

Organizations can self-host MediaWiki on their own infrastructure at no software cost, or use third-party MediaWiki hosting providers that charge for managed hosting, though these are separate commercial services rather than official MediaWiki pricing.

features.title: Wikitext markup and editing
features.description: Uses a lightweight markup language for authoring pages, with optional WYSIWYG editing via the VisualEditor extension.

features.title: Full revision history
features.description: Tracks every edit to every page, allowing changes to be reviewed, compared, and reverted.

features.title: Granular user permissions
features.description: Supports detailed user rights and roles for controlling who can view, edit, or administer content.

features.title: Discussion pages
features.description: Provides dedicated talk pages for collaborative discussion about article content and edits.

features.title: Structured data via Wikibase
features.description: The Wikibase extension adds structured, queryable data support, as used by Wikidata.

features.title: Advanced search via CirrusSearch
features.description: Integrates Elasticsearch for fast, advanced full-text search across large wikis.

features.title: Extensible via extensions and skins
features.description: A large ecosystem of extensions and skins allows deep customization of functionality and appearance.

features.title: Proven scalability
features.description: Powers Wikipedia and its sister projects, demonstrating the software's ability to handle very large-scale traffic and content volumes.

pros: Free and open source with no licensing cost
Proven at massive scale by powering Wikipedia and its sister projects
Extremely mature and extensive extension and skin ecosystem
Strong built-in collaboration features like revision history and discussion pages
Backed by the Wikimedia Foundation and a large, active developer community
cons: Can be complex to install, configure, and maintain compared to simpler wiki tools
Default visual design feels dated without applying custom skins
Wikitext markup has a learning curve for non-technical users, despite VisualEditor
Self-hosting requires ongoing server maintenance and security updates
No official managed hosting from the MediaWiki project itself

faqs.question: What is MediaWiki used for?
faqs.answer: MediaWiki is used to build collaboratively editable wikis, most notably powering Wikipedia and its sister projects, as well as thousands of independent corporate, community, and personal wikis.

faqs.question: Is MediaWiki free?
faqs.answer: Yes, MediaWiki is free and open source, licensed under the GNU General Public License version 2 or later, with no cost to download, install, or modify.

faqs.question: Who maintains MediaWiki?
faqs.answer: MediaWiki is maintained primarily by the Wikimedia Foundation along with a large community of volunteer developers.

faqs.question: What is the difference between MediaWiki and Wikipedia?
faqs.answer: MediaWiki is the underlying software; Wikipedia is one of many websites (and the most famous one) that runs on that software.

faqs.question: Can I add features to MediaWiki?
faqs.answer: Yes, MediaWiki supports a large ecosystem of extensions and skins that add functionality such as structured data (Wikibase), WYSIWYG editing (VisualEditor), and advanced search (CirrusSearch).

faqs.question: What are alternatives to MediaWiki?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include Confluence, DokuWiki, TikiWiki, BookStack, and more modern collaborative workspace tools like Notion, depending on the use case.

use_cases.title: Public knowledge encyclopedias
use_cases.description: MediaWiki powers Wikipedia and its sister projects, serving as the reference implementation for large-scale public wikis.

use_cases.title: Internal corporate knowledge bases
use_cases.description: Organizations self-host MediaWiki to document internal processes, policies, and technical knowledge collaboratively.
use_cases.audience: Enterprise IT and knowledge management teams

use_cases.title: Community and fan wikis
use_cases.description: Communities use MediaWiki to build collaboratively edited reference sites around shared interests or topics.

enrichment.short_description: Medusa (MedusaJS) is an open-source, headless commerce platform built on TypeScript and Node.js that gives developers a modular framework for building custom e-commerce, B2B, and marketplace applications.
enrichment.pricing_model: Open Source with paid Cloud hosting
enrichment.starting_price: Free (self-hosted, MIT license) or from $29 per month on Medusa Cloud
enrichment.seo_title: Medusa (MedusaJS) Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Medusa (MedusaJS) is an open-source headless commerce platform for developers. Compare its features, self-hosted and Cloud pricing, and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Medusa, commonly known as MedusaJS, is an open-source headless commerce platform founded in 2021 in Copenhagen, Denmark, by Sebastian Rindom, Oliver Juhl, and Nicklas Gellner. The company raised an $8 million seed round in 2022 led by Dawn Capital, part of roughly $9 million in total venture funding raised across its early rounds, and has continued to grow its team and platform since. Medusa is built on TypeScript and Node.js and is released under the MIT License, meaning the core platform is completely free to self-host with no license fees, per-transaction charges, or GMV-based taxes. Architecturally, Medusa provides a modular commerce framework of composable building blocks, such as product catalog, cart, order, pricing, promotion, inventory, and fulfillment modules, that developers can use and extend without having to rebuild core commerce logic from scratch, positioning it as a foundation for building custom storefronts, B2B commerce portals, multi-vendor marketplaces, point-of-sale systems, and service-based commerce applications rather than a one-size-fits-all out-of-the-box store like Shopify. In addition to the free, self-hosted open-source core, the company monetizes through Medusa Cloud, a managed hosting offering with published plans including Develop starting from $29 per month, Launch from $99 per month, Scale from $299 per month, and a custom-priced Enterprise tier, with usage-based add-ons for items such as preview environments, compute hours, data transfer, storage, and additional seats. Medusa's target audience is primarily developers and technical teams, ranging from solo founders and agencies building client stores to larger, more complex brands and marketplaces that need deep customization beyond what typical SaaS e-commerce platforms allow. Its main competitors and alternatives include Shopify (particularly Shopify Plus and Hydrogen for headless use cases), commercetools, Saleor, Vendure, BigCommerce, and Spree Commerce, with Medusa differentiating itself through its modern JavaScript and TypeScript-first developer experience, fully open-source and self-hostable core with no vendor lock-in, modular architecture designed for deep customization, and, more recently, an emphasis on being agent- and AI-workflow-friendly for building commerce experiences that integrate with automated or AI-driven purchasing flows. Because the core software is free and open source, adoption spans everything from individual developers experimenting with headless commerce to companies running production storefronts, with Medusa Cloud offered as the official path to managed, production-grade hosting for teams that do not want to operate the infrastructure themselves.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Medusa, widely known as MedusaJS, is an open-source headless commerce platform built for developers who want to build custom e-commerce, B2B, or marketplace applications without starting from scratch. Founded in 2021 in Copenhagen, Denmark, by Sebastian Rindom, Oliver Juhl, and Nicklas Gellner, the company raised an $8 million seed round in 2022 led by Dawn Capital.

The core platform is built on TypeScript and Node.js and released under the MIT License, making it free to self-host with no licensing fees, transaction fees, or gross-merchandise-value-based charges, distinguishing it from many commercial e-commerce platforms.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Medusa provides a modular commerce framework made up of composable building blocks, including product, cart, order, pricing, promotion, inventory, and fulfillment modules, that developers can combine and extend to support advanced B2B, direct-to-consumer, marketplace, or point-of-sale use cases.

Because it is API-first and headless, Medusa can power any frontend, custom storefronts, mobile apps, or even AI-driven commerce agents, without being tied to a specific presentation layer, giving development teams full control over the customer experience.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Medusa's core platform is free and open source under the MIT License, so teams can self-host it on their own infrastructure at no licensing cost.

For teams that prefer managed hosting, Medusa Cloud offers published plans starting with Develop from $29 per month, Launch from $99 per month, and Scale from $299 per month, plus a custom-priced Enterprise tier, with usage-based add-ons for compute, storage, data transfer, and additional seats.

features.title: Modular commerce framework
features.description: Provides composable modules for product, cart, order, pricing, promotion, inventory, and fulfillment logic that developers can extend.

features.title: Headless, API-first architecture
features.description: Decouples the commerce backend from the frontend, allowing any storefront, app, or AI agent to consume the same commerce API.

features.title: TypeScript and Node.js foundation
features.description: Built on a modern, widely-used JavaScript stack familiar to most web development teams.

features.title: B2B and marketplace support
features.description: Includes primitives for complex B2B commerce, multi-vendor marketplaces, and distributor platforms.

features.title: Open-source core with MIT license
features.description: The full platform is free to self-host with no license fees or per-transaction charges.

features.title: Medusa Cloud managed hosting
features.description: Offers an official managed hosting option with tiered plans for teams that do not want to operate their own infrastructure.

features.title: Extensible plugin and integration ecosystem
features.description: Supports plugins and integrations for payments, shipping, search, and other commerce services.

features.title: Point-of-sale and service commerce support
features.description: Modular architecture extends beyond typical online storefronts to POS and service-based business models.

pros: Core platform is completely free and open source with no license or transaction fees
Modern TypeScript and Node.js stack familiar to most development teams
Highly modular architecture suited to deep customization
Supports B2B, marketplace, and POS use cases beyond simple DTC stores
Official Medusa Cloud offers a managed hosting path when teams want to avoid self-ops
cons: Requires development resources; not a drag-and-drop store builder like Shopify
Self-hosting demands infrastructure and DevOps expertise to run in production
Smaller ecosystem of themes and pre-built extensions compared to mature platforms like Shopify
Medusa Cloud pricing can add up with usage-based add-ons at scale
Best suited to technical teams rather than non-technical merchants

faqs.question: What is Medusa (MedusaJS)?
faqs.answer: Medusa is an open-source, headless commerce platform built on TypeScript and Node.js that gives developers modular building blocks for creating custom e-commerce, B2B, and marketplace applications.

faqs.question: Is Medusa free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the core Medusa platform is free and open source under the MIT License, with no license fees or per-transaction charges when self-hosted.

faqs.question: Who founded Medusa?
faqs.answer: Medusa was founded in 2021 in Copenhagen, Denmark, by Sebastian Rindom, Oliver Juhl, and Nicklas Gellner.

faqs.question: What is Medusa Cloud?
faqs.answer: Medusa Cloud is the official managed hosting service for Medusa, with published plans starting at $29 per month for the Develop tier, scaling up to a custom-priced Enterprise tier.

faqs.question: Is Medusa a good Shopify alternative?
faqs.answer: Medusa is a strong alternative for technical teams that need deep customization or headless architecture, whereas Shopify is generally easier for non-technical merchants who want an out-of-the-box store.

faqs.question: What can you build with Medusa?
faqs.answer: Medusa can be used to build custom storefronts, B2B commerce portals, multi-vendor marketplaces, point-of-sale systems, and other commerce applications on top of its modular framework.

use_cases.title: Custom headless storefronts
use_cases.description: Development agencies and brands use Medusa to build fully custom storefronts decoupled from the commerce backend.

use_cases.title: B2B and marketplace platforms
use_cases.description: Companies use Medusa's modular commerce primitives to build complex B2B ordering systems or multi-vendor marketplaces.

use_cases.title: AI and agent-driven commerce
use_cases.description: Teams building AI-driven shopping assistants or automated purchasing flows use Medusa's API-first architecture to power commerce logic.
use_cases.audience: Teams building AI commerce agents and automated workflows

features: Increased compute, storage, and data transfer limits
